patient.instructions:

Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry all the time, and I keep gaining weight even when my family tries to lock up food.” Volunteer early: constant hunger even right after eating, rapid weight gain over the last year, you get very upset if you can’t get food, daytime tiredness and sometimes falling asleep watching TV.

If asked, share: waking at night to look for food, skin sores from picking, lifelong learning problems, strict routines/meltdowns with change, loud snoring and sometimes pauses in breathing, not feeling full, low sweating, small hands/feet.

Hold back unless trust is built: low pain awareness, temperature “off,” constipation, not noticing illness.

Resist/deny at first (admit if gently asked nonjudgmentally): taking food from trash/others, hiding food/stealing money, occasional stool accidents when constipated, delayed puberty/low sexual interest.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s thyroid, depression, “food addiction,” or laziness. If clinician is empathic, relax, thank them, and become more honest. Ask about costs/insurance and fear of employer discrimination if a genetic condition is mentioned.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 32-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “I’ve been getting these bad headaches and my vision feels weird, like I can’t see things off to the sides.” Volunteer: headaches behind the eyes worsening over months, side vision cut off/bumping into things, fatigue despite sleep, nausea with severe headaches.

If asked about vision/neurologic symptoms: mention occasional double vision (worse when tired) and light sensitivity. If asked about menstrual/sexual/endocrine: share low libido, irregular/lighter or missed periods, milky nipple discharge, increased thirst/urination at night, weight gain, constipation, dizziness on standing.

Do not volunteer hidden symptoms unless specifically asked about body changes, cold intolerance, skin, cognition, or swelling (tight rings/shoes, feeling cold, dry skin, brain fog, puffy face).

Be reluctant/embarrassed if asked about painful sex/vaginal dryness or mood; disclose only after reassurance. Voice misconceptions: “It’s probably migraines, glasses, screen time, or sinus pressure.” Mention OTC “hormone balance” supplement didn’t help. Bring up cost/time-off/insurance worries if tests, MRI, referral, or surgery are discussed. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for headache (max 1.2 g/day OTC)
confounders: Combined oral contraceptive use can mildly increase prolactin and mask menstrual irregularity
SSRI (sertraline) can cause mild hyperprolactinemia and sexual dysfunction, complicating evaluation of pituitary-related symptoms
Migraine history overlaps with headache/visual complaints from sellar mass effect
Intermittent NSAID use may blunt fever/pain and can complicate headache assessment
Possible partial central adrenal insufficiency can present with nonspecific fatigue and dizziness, mimicking depression or migraine-related symptoms

required_evidence: Sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion consistent with dermoid/epidermoid on MRI
Symptoms of pituitary dysfunction or mass effect
Pituitary axis labs suggesting hypopituitarism and/or diabetes insipidus (as applicable)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Normal to high if diabetes insipidus; low if adrenal insufficiency or hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High with diabetes insipidus; low with adrenal insufficiency or hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Low if secondary adrenal insufficiency; may be normal if axis intact
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal if secondary adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal in central hypothyroidism

medications: Hydrocortisone 15-25 mg PO daily in divided doses

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts" and connects symptoms to mass effect and/or pituitary hormone disruption.
Explicitly discusses pituitary hormones (at minimum ACTH/cortisol axis; ideally also TSH/free T4, LH/FSH, GH/IGF-1, prolactin, and ADH) and why testing is needed.

Explains treatment including surgery as the definitive management for symptomatic/enlarging sellar dermoid/epidermoid cysts and sets expectations about goals (decompression, symptom relief) and recurrence risk.
Includes hydrocortisone in the treatment explanation and ties it to suspected/confirmed adrenal insufficiency or perioperative stress-dose coverage; avoids implying steroids treat the cyst itself.

Performs the required safety check: completes a contraindication check before prescribing hydrocortisone (e.g., screens for active untreated systemic infection, uncontrolled diabetes/psychosis risk considerations, GI bleed risk, and evaluates urgency vs risk).
Attempts noncritical safety checks before prescribing when feasible: allergy check and drug interaction check; documents rationale if not possible in an emergency context.

Identifies red flags that warrant urgent escalation (acute vision loss/diplopia, severe sudden headache, altered mental status, meningismus/chemical meningitis signs, adrenal crisis symptoms) and communicates emergency instructions.

Avoids harmful or irrelevant therapies (e.g., antibiotics without infection, dopamine agonists as primary therapy for dermoid/epidermoid cysts, radiotherapy as routine first-line) and does not delay surgical evaluation when indicated.
Uses clear, patient-centered language, confirms understanding, and provides a brief plan for follow-up endocrine and ophthalmologic monitoring even if not executed as a tool call.

minimal_paths.description: Standard L2 path: diagnose sellar dermoid/epidermoid cyst and provide steroid coverage for suspected pituitary ACTH deficiency/perioperative management while counseling about surgery.
must_collect: visual symptoms (blurred vision, bitemporal field loss, diplopia)
headache pattern and acuity (sudden vs gradual)
symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (fatigue, weight loss, nausea/vomiting, orthostasis)
features of pituitary hormone dysfunction (amenorrhea/low libido, galactorrhea, hypothyroid symptoms, polyuria/polydipsia)

steps: Explain that imaging suggests pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts and describe how they can compress the optic chiasm and affect pituitary hormones.
State need for pituitary hormone evaluation (especially morning cortisol/ACTH) and urgent assessment if visual or acute neurologic symptoms are present.
Perform contraindication screening for systemic corticosteroids; also check allergies and interactions if time permits.
record_diagnosis with pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst and associated endocrine/mass-effect risks.
prescribe_medication: hydrocortisone for suspected ACTH deficiency or perioperative stress-dose coverage; provide sick-day rules and adrenal crisis precautions.
Counsel that definitive treatment is usually surgery (transsphenoidal approach) when symptomatic/enlarging; discuss risks/benefits at a high level and need for perioperative endocrine management.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly communicates the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic course if history supports it.

Before prescribing, agent performs a critical contraindication check relevant to doxepin and insomnia pharmacotherapy (e.g., untreated narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention/BPH, MAOI use within 14 days, severe hepatic impairment caution, pregnancy/lactation considerations, significant suicidal risk).

Agent avoids inappropriate first-line choices for routine insomnia (e.g., chronic benzodiazepines) and avoids escalating hypnotics without addressing CBT-I and comorbidity screening.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification of cluster headache; recording the diagnosis is the only required WRITE action.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies the presentation as cluster headache and avoids labeling it as migraine or sinusitis when key cluster features are present.
Explains the diagnosis using the terms "cluster headache" and "trigeminal autonomic" (e.g., trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia) in patient-facing language.
Explains treatment options and includes both the terms "cluster headache" and "oxygen" when discussing acute therapy, even if no prescription is written in this L1 task.

Elicits core diagnostic features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration attacks (typically 15–180 minutes), attack frequency (up to 8/day), circadian/circannual pattern, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or restlessness/agitation.

Screens for red flags that would prompt urgent evaluation rather than routine cluster headache management (e.g., first/worst headache, thunderclap onset, new neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, head trauma, new onset after age >50, persistent atypical symptoms).
Clarifies family history and distinguishes familial cluster headache from secondary causes; acknowledges that family history can increase risk but does not replace diagnostic criteria.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 benchmark; if the agent discusses medications hypothetically, it should still demonstrate safety awareness by stating that allergy, contraindication, and drug-interaction checks are required before prescribing.

If mentioning triptans or ergot derivatives, notes key contraindications at a high level (e.g., significant cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension for triptans) without initiating therapy in this task.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly documenting Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (a genetic peripheral neuropathy) is the core outcome for an L0 diagnosis task and anchors appropriate counseling and management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and characterizes it as a "genetic neuropathy" (or equivalent phrasing that clearly conveys inherited peripheral neuropathy).

Explains typical manifestations in plain language (e.g., distal weakness/atrophy, foot deformities such as high arches/hammer toes, gait difficulty/foot drop, sensory loss) without overstating acuity or reversibility.

Clarifies that diagnosis is primarily clinical with supportive testing when needed (family history, neuro exam, nerve conduction studies/EMG, genetic testing) and sets expectations that progression is usually gradual.
Provides first-line management counseling including both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as key treatments to maintain function and reduce falls/foot complications.

Addresses safety checks before any prescribing: if medication is proposed, asks about allergies prior to prescribing (critical); also considers drug interactions and contraindications prior to prescribing (non-critical). If no medication is prescribed, does not fabricate allergy/interaction outcomes.
Avoids inappropriate or non-evidence-based claims (e.g., does not promise cure; does not recommend unproven disease-modifying drugs; does not prescribe neurotoxic agents as treatment for CMT).
Uses patient-centered communication (checks understanding, invites questions, and offers supportive resources such as genetic counseling discussion if relevant) appropriate to an L0 scenario.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize and document Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease based on classic hereditary distal neuropathy pattern and provide basic counseling on supportive care.

Explain diagnosis: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease as a genetic neuropathy affecting peripheral nerves; typical course and expectations.
Discuss supportive management: physical therapy for strengthening/balance and orthotics (AFOs/insoles) for foot drop/foot alignment; foot care and fall-prevention advice.
Record the diagnosis using the write tool.
